description: fetchTon appends one event for the in_msg (line 357) AND one event per out_msg with value > 0 (lines 373-394), all carrying the same `hash` from transaction_id. TON wallet contracts routinely send up to 4 messages from one external (batch sends), and bounce/forward txs have both an inbound and outbound leg. Downstream, TransactionRepository.upsert is keyed by (txHash, addressId) (TransactionRepository.swift:15,42), so the N events overwrite each other and only the last-processed leg survives in SwiftData — a 3-recipient batch send shows as a single row with one arbitrary recipient/amount. In test mode, WalletHomeView.swift:1645 uses ForEach(..., id: \.txHash), so the duplicate ids break SwiftUI diffing (duplicated/garbled rows).
suggestedFix: Disambiguate the hash per leg (e.g. txHash = "\(hash):out:\(index)" / ":in") or aggregate the out_msgs into a single event with the summed amount, and key list identity on a compound id rather than raw txHash.

title: 25-way getTransaction fan-out exceeds the token bucket's bounded wait — spurious rateLimited failures trip the primary endpoint's circuit breaker and silently drop history rows
description: fetch() launches all 25 getTransaction calls concurrently in one TaskGroup (lines 57-69), relying on the per-endpoint RateLimiter to pace them. But sol-mainnet-beta is registered at 10 rps / burst 5 (RPCRegistry.swift:254, RPCEndpoint moderate10), and TokenBucket.consume gives up after 10 loop iterations of ~0.1 s sleeps (~1 s total) and throws RPCError.rateLimited (RateLimiter.swift consume()). With 26 near-simultaneous waiters, the bucket drains instantly and the tail (~10 calls whose token would arrive after ~1 s) deterministically exhausts the bound BEFORE any request is sent. RPCClient counts each such throw as an endpoint failure (recordFailure, RPCClient.swift:270), so a single 25-tx history fetch pushes consecutiveFailures past the threshold of 5 and opens the primary Solana endpoint's circuit breaker for 60 s — also affecting subsequent balance scans. The spilled calls rotate to the publicnode fallback (burst 20); if it too is saturated (multiple Solana accounts, back-to-back refreshes), fetchOne's try? at line 92 swallows the error and the row vanishes — the user sees a nondeterministically incomplete Solana history with no error.
suggestedFix: Bound in-flight concurrency in the TaskGroup (e.g. add at most 4-5 tasks, addTask on each completion), and/or make the rate-limit acquisition wait proportionally to queue depth instead of failing after ~1 s; separately, pre-network rateLimited throws should not count toward the circuit breaker.

title: NEAR and Polkadot balance reads bypass RPCClient: hardcoded single endpoint, no fallback, 60 s default timeout, no rate limiting
description: NEARChainAdapter (line 106) and PolkadotChainAdapter (line 256) POST via URLSession.shared to hardcoded URLs (https://rpc.mainnet.near.org, https://rpc.polkadot.io), bypassing the RPCClient actor entirely. Consequences a user can hit: (1) URLSession.shared's default timeoutIntervalForRequest is 60 s vs the RPCClient session's 10 s — a hung primary RPC stalls the NEAR/DOT slice of a wallet refresh for up to a minute; (2) the registered fallbacks (near.lava.build, onfinality — RPCRegistry.swift:271-294) are never consulted, so any outage of the single hardcoded host silently renders the NEAR/DOT balance as 0 (the catch blocks at lines 119-122 and 263-266 return a zero summary) even while a healthy fallback exists; (3) no rate limiter or circuit breaker applies to these requests.
suggestedFix: Add named-object JSON-RPC params support to RPCClient (callJSONResultData(paramsObject:) already exists — RPCClient.swift:119) and route these two adapters through it so they inherit the 10 s timeout, fallback rotation, and breaker; at minimum configure an explicit short timeout on the URLRequest and iterate the registry's endpoints.

title: Token-2022 SPL balances (PYUSD, AUSD, DUSD, USDG) are never scanned — registry promises per-standard program selection but the scanner only queries the legacy SPL Token program
description: streamRegistryTokens' Solana branch (RealRPCBalanceScanner.swift:340-354) calls SolanaChainAdapter.fetchTokenAccounts(address:), which issues getTokenAccountsByOwner hard-filtered to the legacy SPL Token program id 'TokenkegQfeZyiNwAJbNbGKPFXCWuBvf9Ss623VQ5DA' (SolanaChainAdapter.swift:36) and nothing else. Token-2022 token accounts are owned by the Token-2022 program ('TokenzQd…'), so they are never returned by this RPC call. SolanaTokenRegistry (SolanaTokenRegistry.swift:5-7) explicitly documents the contract: 'Token standard is either splToken (legacy) or splToken2022 — the streaming scanner uses the right SPL Token program id for each', and 4 of the 10 curated mints are tagged .splToken2022: AUSD, DUSD, PYUSD (PayPal USD), USDG. The scanner ignores the standard field entirely — the filter at line 352 (SolanaTokenRegistry.mints[$0.mint] != nil) can never match a Token-2022 mint because the account list never contains one. Concrete failure: a user holding PYUSD on Solana sees no PYUSD row on wallet home and an understated total portfolio value, with no error — the balance silently reads as absent. The same defect hits the custom-token pass at line 625: a user who adds a Token-2022 mint via AddCustomTokenSheet (which explicitly supports splToken2022 via SolanaMintInfo.standard, whose doc comment says 'so the scanner can pick the right token program for balance reads') will never see its balance either.
suggestedFix: Issue two getTokenAccountsByOwner calls — one with programId TokenkegQfeZyiNwAJbNbGKPFXCWuBvf9Ss623VQ5DA and one with TokenzQdBNbLqP5VEhdkAS6EPFLC1PHnBqCXEpPxuEb3UYUg — and merge the results (e.g. add a programId parameter to SolanaChainAdapter.fetchTokenAccounts or a fetchAllTokenAccounts that unions both). Both the registry pass (line 342) and the custom-token pass (line 625) must use the merged result. Alternatively pass each registry entry's Standard through and query only the programs actually needed.

title: Button.primaryLabel / Text.onTint are literal white but the brand accent resolves near-white in dark mode, producing invisible text
description: UniColors.Button.primaryLabel (line 158) and UniColors.Text.onTint (line 79) are hardcoded Color.white, documented as 'text drawn on top of a tinted/accent surface'. But UniColors.Button.primaryTint = Color.accentColor, and AccentColor.colorset resolves to Cloud #F5F5F7 in dark mode (verified in UniApp/Resources/Assets.xcassets/AccentColor.colorset/Contents.json; the file's own Brand doc comment at lines 322-326 states this). White on #F5F5F7 is ~1.02:1 contrast — invisible. Concrete failure: BackupVerifyView.swift:170 paints the selected recovery-phrase word chip with foreground UniColors.Button.primaryLabel on fill UniColors.Button.primaryTint. In dark mode, the moment a user taps a word during recovery-phrase backup verification, its text disappears (white on near-white), so the user cannot read back which word they selected on a security-critical verification surface. UniButton itself is unaffected (glassProminent resolves its own label), which is why the token pair survived until a manual consumer used it as documented.
suggestedFix: Make the on-accent label roles adapt with the accent: define primaryLabel/onTint as an asset-catalog colorset (white in light, Ink #0B0D11 in dark) or Color(uiColor: .systemBackground)-based role so it always contrasts the monochrome accent; audit BrowserQRScanSheet's onTint use (white over camera feed) and give it a dedicated always-white role like Text.onMedia instead.

title: uniHaptic(.error, trigger:) bypasses UniHapticEngine frustration silencing — Rule #10 §J never engages for wrong-PIN errors
description: UniHapticModifier.body (lines 329-345) routes any haptic with a non-nil SensoryFeedback mapping straight to .sensoryFeedback(feedback, trigger:). UniHaptic.error maps to .error (line 245), so the single-shot modifier path never reaches UniHapticEngine.playError() and its 3-errors-in-10s silencing (the directional modifier at lines 359-385 explicitly special-cases .error through the engine for exactly this reason, per its own comment). Grep confirms the only .error haptic call sites in the app are the single-shot form: PinCodeView.swift:146 (.uniHaptic(.error, trigger: errorTrigger) — wrong PIN entry, the canonical Rule #10 §J use case) and BackupVerifyView.swift:81; nothing anywhere calls UniHapticEngine play/fire with .error. Net effect: the documented frustration-silencing behavior is dead code — a user repeatedly mistyping their PIN gets an error buzz on every single attempt, and the engine's error window/counter state is never fed.
suggestedFix: In UniHapticModifier, special-case .error the same way the directional modifier does: route it through UniHapticEngine.shared.play(.error) via onChange instead of the direct sensoryFeedback mapping (or check the engine's silencing state before firing).

title: pendingErrorSilences never expires with the 10-second frustration window — errors hours later are silently swallowed
description: playError() (lines 482-503) prunes recentErrors timestamps older than the 10s window (line 485), but pendingErrorSilences (decremented at lines 487-490) has no time bound. Rule #10 §J states 'State resets after 10s of no errors', yet once the threshold trips and only 1-2 of the silenced errors are consumed, the remaining silence credits persist indefinitely. Concrete scenario (once finding #3 is fixed and errors actually route through the engine): user mistypes their PIN 3 times at 09:00 (third buzz trips the threshold, pendingErrorSilences = 2), then four times taps only 2 more wrong digits over the day — a wrong-PIN attempt hours later produces no error haptic at all, contradicting the documented reset. Currently latent because no call site reaches playError() (see the UniHaptic.swift:331 finding), but it becomes live the moment that routing bug is fixed.
suggestedFix: Record the time the silencing was armed and clear pendingErrorSilences whenever now - lastErrorTime > frustrationWindow at the top of playError(), so silence credits expire together with the window.

title: Native XRP balance always renders 0 — rippled's HTTP API is not JSON-RPC 2.0, RPCClient's id-echo validation rejects every response
description: XRPChainAdapter.fetchAccountSummary routes account_info through RPCClient.callJSONResultData, whose dispatchJSON validates that the response echoes the request id (RPCClient.swift:454-456, responseID returns false when 'id' is absent). rippled's HTTP API does not return an 'id' field — live-verified against both registered endpoints: POST to https://s1.ripple.com:51234 and https://xrplcluster.com with {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":7,...} returns top-level keys ['result','status','type','forwarded','warnings'] — no 'id'. Every call therefore throws .invalidResponse, all 3 ripple endpoints get marked failed, and the catch block at line 44-47 swallows the error and returns ChainAccountSummary(nativeBalance: 0, isUsed: false). Concrete scenario: any user holding XRP sees a 0 XRP balance, every scan, on every endpoint — a wallet showing zero for real funds. (The RLUSD IOU path in RealRPCBalanceScanner.fetchXRPLTokenLines works only because it bypasses RPCClient with a direct URLSession call.)
suggestedFix: Either bypass RPCClient's JSON-RPC envelope for XRPL (direct POST like fetchXRPLTokenLines, validating result.status == "success"), or teach RPCClient to skip id validation for endpoints flagged as rippled-style (rippled ignores jsonrpc/id and returns {result:{...}, status, type}).

title: XRP transaction history is always empty — account_tx fails RPCClient's JSON-RPC id-echo check on every rippled endpoint
description: Same root cause as the XRP balance bug: fetch() calls client.callJSONResultData(chain: .ripple, method: "account_tx", ...), but rippled responses carry no 'id' field (live-verified on s1.ripple.com and xrplcluster.com), so RPCClient.responseID (RPCClient.swift:454-456) rejects every response with .invalidResponse. All three registered XRPL endpoints fail identically, RealRPCTransactionScanner.fetch catches the throw (RealRPCTransactionScanner.swift:181-184) and returns []. Concrete scenario: a user with years of XRP payment history opens the activity feed and sees 'No activity yet' permanently. The entire body of this adapter (lines 31-127) is currently unreachable dead logic.
suggestedFix: Route XRPL calls around the JSON-RPC 2.0 envelope validation (direct rippled-format POST, validate result.status == "success") or add a per-endpoint flag in RPCEndpoint that relaxes the id-echo requirement for rippled-protocol endpoints.

title: TRC-20 feed displays attacker-chosen token symbols verbatim — scam airdrop named 'USDT' renders as real USDT
description: fetchTRC20 trusts token_info.symbol from TronGrid's /transactions/trc20 endpoint (line 126) with no validation against TronTokenRegistry and no contract-address check. TronGrid returns transfers of EVERY TRC-20 contract that touched the address, including spam/scam tokens whose contracts self-declare symbol 'USDT' and decimals 6 — a pervasive, well-documented attack on Tron specifically (fake-USDT airdrops). Concrete scenario: attacker airdrops 10,000 units of a fake token with symbol 'USDT' to the victim; the activity feed renders '+10,000 USDT incoming', indistinguishable from a real Tether transfer (tokenContract is stored but the row's primary label is the symbol). A user selling goods against the feed, or believing a 'refund' arrived, is defrauded. Contrast: the Solana adapter deliberately labels unknown mints 'SPL' (SolanaTransactionAdapter.swift:311-318) to avoid exactly this.
suggestedFix: Resolve the symbol from TronTokenRegistry by contract address (token_info.address); for unknown contracts render a neutral label ('TRC-20' plus truncated contract) instead of the self-declared symbol, mirroring the Solana adapter's knownMintSymbol approach.

title: Stellar feed displays attacker-chosen asset codes — anyone can issue 'USDC' and the feed labels it as USDC
description: Line 48 sets the displayed symbol to op.asset_code verbatim for any non-native payment. On Stellar, anyone can issue an asset with any code; only the (code, issuer) pair identifies an asset. An attacker issues an asset with code 'USDC' from their own account and path-pays dust to the victim (no trustline needed for claimable/path mechanics in some flows, and any historical payment op to the account surfaces here); the feed renders '+1,000 USDC incoming' with the real issuer relegated to tokenContract. Same fraud class as the Tron finding: the user believes real Circle USDC arrived. The legitimate-issuer check is one comparison away — the registry pattern already used for balances elsewhere.
suggestedFix: Maintain a known (asset_code, asset_issuer) allowlist (e.g., Circle's USDC issuer GA5ZSEJYB37JRC5AVCIA5MOP4RHTM335X2KGX3IHOJAPP5RE34K4KZVN); for unknown issuers display a neutral label such as the code plus truncated issuer or 'Unknown asset', never the bare code.

title: Kava outgoing rows show the FEE as the amount and the fee-collector as counterparty — first matching 'transfer' event is the ante-handler fee deduction
description: queryKavaTxs walks raw['events'] and appends a TransactionEvent for the FIRST 'transfer' event whose sender or recipient equals the address, then breaks (lines 536-594). In Cosmos SDK, tx_response.events includes ante-handler events emitted before message execution: fee deduction calls SendCoinsFromAccountToModule, which emits a 'transfer' event from the signer to the fee_collector module account. For any outgoing Kava transaction with a non-zero fee, that fee transfer is the first 'transfer' event matching sender == address, so the row renders amount = the fee (e.g. '−0.005 KAVA') with counterparty = the fee-collector module address, while the actual send amount and the real recipient never display. Incoming rows are unaffected (the sender's fee event involves neither side equal to the viewing address, so the loop continues to the real transfer).
suggestedFix: Skip transfer events whose recipient is the fee_collector module account (kava17xpfvakm2amg962yls6f84z3kell8c5lvkzrsl), or prefer the message-level event (filter by msg_index attribute presence in SDK 0.46+), or pick the transfer event with the largest amount involving the address instead of the first.

title: Legacy spl-token transfer fallback displays raw base units when token-balance metadata is missing (1,000,000x overstatement for USDC)
description: For legacy 'transfer' instructions (no tokenAmount object) decimals is set to 0 at line 164. When meta.preTokenBalances/postTokenBalances are absent or don't cover the wallet, the fallback branch (lines 190-199) sets resolvedDecimals = decimals = 0, so the amount at line 200 is the raw integer amount undivided: a 1-USDC transfer (raw 1000000) renders as '1,000,000 USDC'. Concrete scenario: a malicious or pruned RPC node (the adapter rotates across public endpoints) returns a getTransaction response without token-balance meta for an incoming USDT/USDC transfer — the feed shows a six-orders-of-magnitude inflated incoming amount, the same fraud-enabling display class as the partial-payment issue. The transferChecked path is safe (decimals carried in the instruction).
suggestedFix: When decimals cannot be resolved (legacy transfer + no balance meta), skip the event or resolve decimals via SolanaTokenRegistry.mints[mint]?.decimals / getMint, rather than rendering raw base units.

title: fetchMintInfo decodes any >=82-byte account as an SPL mint — no owner-program or is_initialized validation; garbage decimals (up to 255) flow into Add Custom Token
description: fetchMintInfo (lines 97-130) reads getAccountInfo for a user-supplied address and accepts any account whose data is >= 82 bytes, decoding byte 44 as decimals. It reads the owner field only to pick splToken vs splToken2022 via hasPrefix("TokenzQ") — any non-token-program account (a Metaplex metadata account, a BPF program data account, a stake account) silently classifies as .splToken instead of being rejected, and is_initialized (byte 45) is never checked. Concrete scenario: a user pastes a Solana account address that isn't a mint into Add Custom Token (AddCustomTokenSheet.swift:405); the sheet confidently shows a decoded 'decimals' value that is actually an arbitrary data byte (possible values up to 255, where 10^255 overflows Decimal into NaN when used as a scale factor), and the token is added with garbage decimals so any later balance/amount math is wrong. Validating the owner is one string comparison against the two token program ids.
suggestedFix: Throw .decodingFailed unless value['owner'] equals TokenkegQfeZyiNwAJbNbGKPFXCWuBvf9Ss623VQ5DA or TokenzQdBNbLqP5VEhdkAS6EPFLC1PHnBqCXEpPxuEb exactly (not hasPrefix), and require bytes[45] == 1 (is_initialized).
